title: Johnson & Johnson — Immunology — knowledge doc psoriasis
html:
Market Context: ICOTYDE (icotrokinra) in Plaque Psoriasis

Product: ICOTYDE (icotrokinra) Manufacturer: Johnson & Johnson Innovative Medicine (Janssen Biotech) Indication: Moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis in adults and pediatric patients >=12 years (>=40 kg) who are candidates for systemic therapy or phototherapy Document scope: Product-level competitive/clinical landscape (not wave-specific) Last updated: April 2026

1. Disease Landscape

1.1 Epidemiology

  • Plaque psoriasis is the most common form of psoriasis (~80-90% of cases), affecting ~8 million Americans
  • Moderate-to-severe disease (requiring systemic therapy): ~3 million patients in the US
  • Biologic-eligible population: ~500,000-800,000 patients currently; many more are underserved (undertreated with topicals when systemic therapy is indicated)
  • Significant quality-of-life impact: Visible plaques cause stigma, depression (~25-30% comorbid), sleep disruption, arthritis (PsA in ~30%), and cardiovascular disease risk

1.2 Why Oral Matters

The psoriasis treatment landscape has been dominated by injectable biologics for over a decade. While biologics achieve high efficacy (PASI 90+ rates of 60-85%), the systemic therapy market has a fundamental access problem:

  • Injection aversion: ~10-20% of patients eligible for biologics decline due to fear of needles or preference for oral therapy
  • Patient inertia on topicals: Many patients remain on inadequate topical therapy because the "next step" requires injections
  • Oral gap: Prior oral systemics (methotrexate, cyclosporine, apremilast) have limited efficacy (~PASI 75 rates of 30-40%) and/or significant side effects — they don't match biologic efficacy
  • ICOTYDE fills this gap: The first targeted oral therapy that delivers biologic-level efficacy in plaque psoriasis

1.3 Standard Treatment Algorithm (Updated for ICOTYDE)

Mild psoriasis: Topicals (corticosteroids, vitamin D, calcineurin inhibitors)
  |
  v
Moderate-to-severe psoriasis:
  |
  ├── Phototherapy (UVB, excimer laser)
  |
  ├── Traditional oral systemics:
  |     ├── Methotrexate (cheap, but hepatotoxicity, teratogenicity, monitoring burden)
  |     ├── Cyclosporine (effective but nephrotoxicity limits long-term use)
  |     ├── Acitretin (retinoid; limited efficacy as monotherapy)
  |     └── Apremilast (Otezla, PDE4 inhibitor — well-tolerated but modest efficacy, PASI 75 ~30-40%)
  |
  ├── **ICOTYDE (oral IL-23 receptor antagonist) — NEW, March 2026**
  |     └── Biologic-class efficacy in an oral tablet — positioned between traditional orals and injectable biologics
  |
  └── Injectable biologics:
        ├── Anti-IL-23: TREMFYA (guselkumab), Skyrizi (risankizumab)
        ├── Anti-IL-17: Cosentyx (secukinumab), Taltz (ixekizumab), Bimzelx (bimekizumab)
        ├── Anti-TNF: adalimumab (biosimilars), etanercept, infliximab, certolizumab
        └── Anti-IL-12/23: Stelara (ustekinumab, biosimilars)

1.4 Unmet Needs

  1. Oral option with biologic-class efficacy: Prior to ICOTYDE, patients had to choose between convenient-but-modest orals or effective-but-injectable biologics. ICOTYDE bridges this gap
  2. Earlier systemic therapy adoption: Many patients remain undertreated on topicals; an oral option may lower the threshold for systemic therapy initiation
  3. Injection-averse patients: Significant population currently excluded from optimal therapy due to needle phobia
  4. Simplification of treatment: Oral daily dosing at home eliminates clinic visits, injection training, and cold-chain logistics
  5. Pediatric oral option: Approved for >=12 years — a meaningful expansion given limited pediatric psoriasis options

2. Product Profiles

2.1 ICOTYDE (icotrokinra) — Johnson & Johnson

Mechanism of Action: ICOTYDE is a first-in-class oral cyclic peptide that selectively antagonizes the IL-23 receptor, blocking the interaction between IL-23 (a key driver of psoriatic inflammation) and its receptor complex (IL-23R/IL-12Rbeta1). By targeting the receptor rather than the cytokine itself, ICOTYDE provides pathway-specific blockade equivalent to injectable anti-IL-23 antibodies (TREMFYA, Skyrizi), but in an oral formulation.

The cyclic peptide structure gives ICOTYDE oral bioavailability — a significant pharmacological achievement, as biologics targeting cytokine pathways have historically required parenteral administration.

Key Efficacy Data:

Pivotal Phase 3 Trials:

  • PASI 90 response rates: Comparable to injectable IL-23 inhibitors — biologic-class skin clearance from an oral tablet
  • PASI 100 (complete clearance): Demonstrated in a meaningful proportion of patients
  • Durability: Sustained response demonstrated through maintenance phase
  • Onset of action: Response observed within the first few weeks; clinically meaningful improvement by week 8-12
  • Pediatric (>=12 years): Included in the approved label

Safety Profile:

  • Detailed safety data from pivotal trials — verify specific AEs against prescribing information once published
  • Expected to share the favorable safety class effect of IL-23 pathway inhibition (no boxed warnings for injectable IL-23 agents)
  • Oral administration eliminates injection site reactions
  • Monitor for infections consistent with immunomodulation

Dosing/Administration:

  • Oral tablet, daily — simplest possible administration
  • At-home, no clinic visits, no injection training, no cold chain
  • No REMS, no monitoring requirements beyond standard of care for systemic psoriasis therapy

Access/Reimbursement:

  • FDA approval: March 2026
  • J&J peak revenue projection: >$5 billion annually — reflecting the enormous oral-switch opportunity
  • Launch phase: Payer coverage building; specialty pharmacy or standard pharmacy distribution TBD
  • Patient support via Janssen CarePath

2.2 Apremilast (Otezla) — Amgen (acquired from Celgene/BMS)

Mechanism of Action: PDE4 inhibitor — oral, broad anti-inflammatory mechanism. Not targeted to IL-23 pathway specifically.

Key Efficacy Data:

  • PASI 75 at week 16: ~30-33% (ESTEEM-1) — significantly lower than biologics or ICOTYDE
  • PASI 90: ~10-15%
  • Well-tolerated; GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea) in early weeks

Market Position:

  • The only prior oral targeted therapy for psoriasis with meaningful market share
  • Modest efficacy limits it to mild-to-moderate psoriasis or biologic-intolerant patients
  • ICOTYDE directly displaces Otezla by offering biologic-class efficacy in the same oral format
  • Revenue declining as biologic competition intensifies; ICOTYDE will accelerate this

2.3 Deucravacitinib (Sotyktu) — BMS

Mechanism of Action: Oral TYK2 inhibitor — allosteric, selective. Targets TYK2 (which mediates IL-23 and IL-12 signaling) without broadly inhibiting other JAK family members.

Key Efficacy Data:

  • POETYK PSO-1 (Phase 3): PASI 75: ~58% at week 16 vs. ~35% (apremilast) — superior to Otezla
  • PASI 90: ~37%
  • Positioned as "better than Otezla, safer than JAK inhibitors"

Market Position:

  • Closest oral competitor to ICOTYDE in psoriasis
  • Efficacy is better than Otezla but still substantially below injectable biologics and likely below ICOTYDE
  • No boxed warnings (unlike JAK inhibitors) — safety differentiation vs. JAK class
  • BMS promoting aggressively in dermatology

Relevance to ICOTYDE:

  • Direct oral-vs-oral competitor. The question is whether Sotyktu's efficacy ceiling (~PASI 90 of 37%) can compete with ICOTYDE's biologic-level PASI 90 rates
  • If ICOTYDE delivers PASI 90 >60%, Sotyktu's positioning is significantly weakened

2.4 Injectable IL-23 Inhibitors (TREMFYA, Skyrizi)

Relevance:

  • TREMFYA and Skyrizi are J&J and AbbVie's flagship injectable IL-23 agents (detailed in separate market context doc)
  • ICOTYDE and TREMFYA are J&J portfolio siblings — same pathway, different modalities
  • ICOTYDE may cannibalize some TREMFYA psoriasis share (patients switching from injection to oral), but J&J retains the revenue
  • TREMFYA retains differentiation via GI (UC, CD) and PsA indications — ICOTYDE is psoriasis-only currently

2.5 Injectable IL-17 Inhibitors (Cosentyx, Taltz, Bimzelx)

Relevance:

  • IL-17 inhibitors offer the highest skin clearance rates (Bimzelx PASI 100 ~60-70%)
  • ICOTYDE competes on convenience (oral) rather than pure efficacy ceiling
  • IL-17 inhibitors cannot be used in patients with IBD comorbidity — ICOTYDE (IL-23 pathway) has no such restriction
  • The oral convenience of ICOTYDE may attract patients who would otherwise be prescribed IL-17 agents

3. Competitive Dynamics

3.1 Oral Psoriasis Therapies

Attribute ICOTYDE Sotyktu (deucravacitinib) Otezla (apremilast)
Mechanism Oral IL-23R antagonist Oral TYK2 inhibitor Oral PDE4 inhibitor
PASI 90 Biologic-class (>>50%) ~37% ~10-15%
PASI 75 High ~58% ~30-33%
Administration Oral daily Oral daily Oral BID
Boxed warning No (expected) No No
GI side effects TBD Mild Significant (early)
Approved March 2026 December 2022 March 2014
IBD safe Yes (IL-23 mechanism) Yes Yes
Peak revenue est. >$5B (J&J) ~$2-4B (BMS) ~$2.5B (declining)

3.2 Oral vs. Injectable Positioning

Attribute ICOTYDE (oral IL-23) TREMFYA/Skyrizi (injectable IL-23) Bimzelx (injectable IL-17A/F)
Administration Oral daily SubQ Q8-12W SubQ Q4W
Convenience Highest Moderate Moderate
PASI 100 TBD (high expected) ~44-60% ~60-70%
Multi-TA (GI, PsA) Psoriasis only Yes (psoriasis + PsA + UC + CD) PsA only (no GI)
Patient preference Needle-averse, convenience-seekers Patients OK with injection, want proven durability Patients wanting highest clearance

3.3 Guideline Status

  • ICOTYDE is expected to receive rapid guideline inclusion given its first-in-class oral IL-23R mechanism and biologic-level efficacy
  • AAD/NPF Joint Guidelines: Likely to position ICOTYDE as a first-line systemic option alongside injectable biologics — the first oral agent to achieve this positioning
  • May fundamentally restructure the treatment algorithm by creating a legitimate "oral biologic-equivalent" step before injectable biologics

3.4 Key Competitive Differentiators for ICOTYDE

Strengths:

  1. First-in-class oral IL-23 receptor antagonist — no other oral therapy delivers biologic-class IL-23 pathway blockade
  2. Biologic-level efficacy in a pill — the core breakthrough; eliminates the efficacy-convenience trade-off
  3. $5B+ peak revenue opportunity — reflects the enormous unmet demand for an effective oral psoriasis therapy
  4. No injection burden: Eliminates needles, cold chain, clinic visits, injection training
  5. J&J portfolio complementarity: TREMFYA (injectable multi-TA) + ICOTYDE (oral psoriasis) = comprehensive IL-23 portfolio
  6. Pediatric inclusion (>=12 years): An oral biologic-class option for adolescents is a significant differentiator

Vulnerabilities:

  1. Psoriasis-only indication (currently): TREMFYA and Skyrizi cover PsA and IBD; ICOTYDE cannot address patients with significant joint disease or GI comorbidity
  2. New-to-market: No long-term safety data or real-world evidence; dermatologists will want to see post-marketing safety before switching established biologic patients
  3. Pricing and payer coverage: Payer strategy during launch is critical; if priced at biologic levels, payers may resist covering an oral when cheaper oral alternatives (Otezla, Sotyktu) exist
  4. Daily dosing compliance: Unlike Q8-12W injectable biologics, ICOTYDE requires daily adherence — a compliance concern for some patients
  5. Sotyktu competition: BMS will aggressively defend the oral psoriasis space; expect head-to-head messaging on efficacy, safety, and physician familiarity
  6. Injectable biologic stickiness: Patients well-controlled on TREMFYA or Skyrizi may not switch; ICOTYDE's primary growth is from new-to-systemic patients and injection-averse populations

4. Market Context

4.1 Market Size and Segmentation

  • US psoriasis systemic therapy market: ~$15-18B annually
  • Injectable biologic share: ~$12-14B (dominant)
  • Oral systemic share: ~$2-4B (Otezla + Sotyktu + traditional orals)
  • Biologic-eligible but untreated/undertreated: ~1-2 million patients — ICOTYDE's biggest growth opportunity
  • Injection-averse patients: ~10-20% of biologic-eligible population — a captive audience for oral IL-23

4.2 Target Prescriber Universe

  • Dermatologists: ~12,000 in the US; ~3,000-5,000 actively prescribe systemic psoriasis therapy
  • High-volume psoriasis specialists: ~500-1,000 at academic centers and psoriasis-focused practices
  • Primary care physicians: Manage mild psoriasis but refer moderate-to-severe; an oral option may encourage some to initiate systemic therapy without referral
  • Key dynamic: ICOTYDE lowers the prescribing threshold — dermatologists who hesitated to start patients on injectables may feel more comfortable starting with an oral biologic-class agent

4.3 Key Market Dynamics

  1. Oral-switch wave is coming: ICOTYDE creates a new category — oral biologic-equivalent therapy. Expect significant patient interest and pull demand. The launch trajectory will be one of the fastest in psoriasis history
  2. J&J dual-brand strategy (TREMFYA + ICOTYDE): J&J must balance two products targeting the same pathway. Expect positioning as: ICOTYDE for oral-preference/new-to-systemic patients; TREMFYA for patients wanting less frequent dosing and multi-domain coverage (PsA, IBD)
  3. BMS counter-attack with Sotyktu: BMS will position Sotyktu as the proven oral option with 3+ years of data vs. ICOTYDE's new launch. Expect aggressive head-to-head messaging on safety track record
  4. Payer dynamics will shape launch speed: If payers require step-through Otezla/Sotyktu before ICOTYDE, the ramp is slower. If ICOTYDE achieves first-line oral coverage, the ramp is explosive
  5. Real-world evidence demand: Dermatologists want post-marketing data before broadly switching; KOL early adopters will generate the first RWE signal

4.4 Prescribing Decision Drivers

  1. Efficacy vs. oral comparators: If ICOTYDE delivers PASI 90 rates >>50% (vs. Sotyktu ~37%, Otezla ~10-15%), the efficacy gap is enormous and the primary prescribing driver
  2. Patient preference for oral: For new-to-systemic patients, oral is overwhelmingly preferred; for injection-averse patients, ICOTYDE removes the last barrier
  3. Safety profile: Long-term safety data will take time to accumulate; dermatologists will weigh the known safety of injectable IL-23 vs. the new oral profile
  4. Payer coverage and cost: Formulary positioning, prior authorization requirements, and copay relative to injectables will influence adoption speed
  5. Multi-domain disease: Patients with PsA or IBD comorbidity may still need TREMFYA/Skyrizi for multi-domain coverage; ICOTYDE alone won't address joint or GI disease
  6. Compliance: Daily pill vs. Q8-12W injection — some physicians prefer the guaranteed adherence of infrequent injections; others prefer the patient autonomy of oral dosing

5. Pipeline and Future Expansion

5.1 ICOTYDE Pipeline

Program Indication Status Expected Impact
Plaque psoriasis Moderate-to-severe (adults + adolescents) Approved March 2026 Active launch
Psoriatic arthritis Active PsA Expected Phase 3 Would expand to PsA, competing directly with Sotyktu and injectable biologics
Additional indications UC, CD, other IL-23-mediated diseases TBD If oral IL-23R antagonism works in GI, ICOTYDE becomes transformative

5.2 Competitor Pipeline

Agent Company Mechanism Status Expected Impact
Sotyktu (deucravacitinib) BMS Oral TYK2 inhibitor Approved; expanding Primary oral competitor; inferior efficacy likely
Oral IL-17 agents Multiple Various oral modulations Phase 2-3 Could create another oral biologic-class competitor
Bimzelx label expansions UCB IL-17A/F Expanding indications Maintains injectable efficacy ceiling advantage
Next-gen oral peptides Various Multiple targets Early Platform validation from ICOTYDE may accelerate oral biologic field

6. Key Themes for Tracking

6.1 Primary Promotional Narratives

Brand Core Message Supporting Data
ICOTYDE "First oral biologic-class therapy for psoriasis — biologic efficacy in a pill" Biologic-level PASI 90 rates; first oral IL-23R antagonist
Sotyktu "Proven oral option with 3+ years of real-world safety data" POETYK data; established safety track record
Otezla "Well-known, well-tolerated oral option" 10+ years on market; declining relevance
TREMFYA "Broadest IL-23 portfolio — psoriasis, PsA, UC, CD" Multi-domain coverage advantage
Skyrizi "Best-in-class IL-23 with Q12W dosing" Highest injectable IL-23 efficacy and longest interval

6.2 Anticipated Competitive Battlegrounds

  1. ICOTYDE vs. Sotyktu efficacy gap: The defining competition in oral psoriasis. If ICOTYDE delivers PASI 90 rates 2-3x higher than Sotyktu, the clinical differentiation is decisive. Track head-to-head physician perception and prescribing intent
  2. Oral vs. injectable preference evolution: Track how many patients and physicians choose oral ICOTYDE over injectable biologics — is it primarily new-to-systemic patients, or are established biologic patients switching?
  3. J&J TREMFYA → ICOTYDE cannibalization: Monitor whether TREMFYA psoriasis patients switch to ICOTYDE, and whether J&J's dual-brand messaging effectively segments the two products
  4. Payer and access dynamics: Track formulary positioning, prior authorization requirements, and step therapy mandates — these will define launch speed
  5. Safety signal monitoring: As the first oral IL-23R antagonist, any unexpected safety signals will disproportionately impact ICOTYDE adoption. Track dermatologist safety perceptions wave-over-wave
  6. Real-world evidence accumulation: First RWE from early adopters will be critical for broader adoption. Track KOL sentiment and published case series

7. Claims Requiring Verification

High Confidence

  • ICOTYDE (icotrokinra) FDA approved March 2026
  • First-in-class oral IL-23 receptor antagonist (cyclic peptide)
  • Approved for moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis (adults + pediatric >=12 years, >=40 kg)
  • J&J projects >$5 billion peak annual revenue
  • Mechanism: selective oral IL-23 receptor antagonist

Moderate Confidence

  • Specific PASI 90 and PASI 100 rates from pivotal trials — verify against FDA label and published trial data
  • Safety profile details — verify against prescribing information once widely available
  • Dosing regimen (assumed daily oral) — verify against prescribing information
  • Sotyktu PASI 90 ~37% and PASI 75 ~58% — verify against specific trial and timepoint
  • Otezla PASI 75 ~30-33% — verify against ESTEEM trials

Lower Confidence

  • ICOTYDE PsA and GI pipeline plans — not yet publicly confirmed; speculative based on mechanism
  • Biologic-eligible but untreated population ~1-2 million — composite industry estimate
  • Payer coverage strategy and formulary positioning — evolving during launch
  • Injection-averse population ~10-20% — varies by survey methodology
  • ICOTYDE daily dosing compliance impact — no real-world adherence data yet
  • Long-term safety profile — limited to pivotal trial duration; post-marketing data needed

This document serves as the product-level competitive/clinical foundation for all tracking studies related to the ICOTYDE launch (ICOTYDE PET is flagged as CRITICAL in J&J planning). Distinct from the TREMFYA market context, which covers the injectable IL-23 portfolio across Derm, Gastro, and Rheum. Not wave-versioned — one file per therapeutic area.

Back to Home | TA Knowledge Hub

1. Disease Landscape
1.1 Epidemiology

Plaque psoriasis is the most common form of psoriasis (~80-90% of cases), affecting ~8 million Americans
Moderate-to-severe disease (requiring systemic therapy): ~3 million patients in the US
Biologic-eligible population: ~500,000-800,000 patients currently; many more are underserved (undertreated with topicals when systemic therapy is indicated)
Significant quality-of-life impact: Visible plaques cause stigma, depression (~25-30% comorbid), sleep disruption, arthritis (PsA in ~30%), and cardiovascular disease risk

1.2 Why Oral Matters
The psoriasis treatment landscape has been dominated by injectable biologics for over a decade. While biologics achieve high efficacy (PASI 90+ rates of 60-85%), the systemic therapy market has a fundamental access problem:

Injection aversion: ~10-20% of patients eligible for biologics decline due to fear of needles or preference for oral therapy
Patient inertia on topicals: Many patients remain on inadequate topical therapy because the "next step" requires injections
Oral gap: Prior oral systemics (methotrexate, cyclosporine, apremilast) have limited efficacy (~PASI 75 rates of 30-40%) and/or significant side effects — they don't match biologic efficacy
ICOTYDE fills this gap: The first targeted oral therapy that delivers biologic-level efficacy in plaque psoriasis

1.3 Standard Treatment Algorithm (Updated for ICOTYDE)
Mild psoriasis: Topicals (corticosteroids, vitamin D, calcineurin inhibitors)
  |
  v
Moderate-to-severe psoriasis:
  |
  ├── Phototherapy (UVB, excimer laser)
  |
  ├── Traditional oral systemics:
  |     ├── Methotrexate (cheap, but hepatotoxicity, teratogenicity, monitoring burden)
  |     ├── Cyclosporine (effective but nephrotoxicity limits long-term use)
  |     ├── Acitretin (retinoid; limited efficacy as monotherapy)
  |     └── Apremilast (Otezla, PDE4 inhibitor — well-tolerated but modest efficacy, PASI 75 ~30-40%)
  |
  ├── **ICOTYDE (oral IL-23 receptor antagonist) — NEW, March 2026**

  |
  └── Injectable biologics:
        ├── Anti-IL-23: TREMFYA (guselkumab), Skyrizi (risankizumab)
        ├── Anti-IL-17: Cosentyx (secukinumab), Taltz (ixekizumab), Bimzelx (bimekizumab)
        ├── Anti-TNF: adalimumab (biosimilars), etanercept, infliximab, certolizumab
        └── Anti-IL-12/23: Stelara (ustekinumab, biosimilars)

1.4 Unmet Needs

Oral option with biologic-class efficacy: Prior to ICOTYDE, patients had to choose between convenient-but-modest orals or effective-but-injectable biologics. ICOTYDE bridges this gap
Earlier systemic therapy adoption: Many patients remain undertreated on topicals; an oral option may lower the threshold for systemic therapy initiation
Injection-averse patients: Significant population currently excluded from optimal therapy due to needle phobia
Simplification of treatment: Oral daily dosing at home eliminates clinic visits, injection training, and cold-chain logistics
Pediatric oral option: Approved for >=12 years — a meaningful expansion given limited pediatric psoriasis options


2. Product Profiles
2.1 ICOTYDE (icotrokinra) — Johnson & Johnson
Mechanism of Action:
ICOTYDE is a first-in-class oral cyclic peptide that selectively antagonizes the IL-23 receptor, blocking the interaction between IL-23 (a key driver of psoriatic inflammation) and its receptor complex (IL-23R/IL-12Rbeta1). By targeting the receptor rather than the cytokine itself, ICOTYDE provides pathway-specific blockade equivalent to injectable anti-IL-23 antibodies (TREMFYA, Skyrizi), but in an oral formulation.
The cyclic peptide structure gives ICOTYDE oral bioavailability — a significant pharmacological achievement, as biologics targeting cytokine pathways have historically required parenteral administration.
Key Efficacy Data:
Pivotal Phase 3 Trials:

PASI 90 response rates: Comparable to injectable IL-23 inhibitors — biologic-class skin clearance from an oral tablet
PASI 100 (complete clearance): Demonstrated in a meaningful proportion of patients
Durability: Sustained response demonstrated through maintenance phase
Onset of action: Response observed within the first few weeks; clinically meaningful improvement by week 8-12
Pediatric (>=12 years): Included in the approved label

Safety Profile:

Detailed safety data from pivotal trials — verify specific AEs against prescribing information once published
Expected to share the favorable safety class effect of IL-23 pathway inhibition (no boxed warnings for injectable IL-23 agents)
Oral administration eliminates injection site reactions
Monitor for infections consistent with immunomodulation

Dosing/Administration:

Oral tablet, daily — simplest possible administration
At-home, no clinic visits, no injection training, no cold chain
No REMS, no monitoring requirements beyond standard of care for systemic psoriasis therapy

Access/Reimbursement:

FDA approval: March 2026
J&J peak revenue projection: >$5 billion annually — reflecting the enormous oral-switch opportunity
Launch phase: Payer coverage building; specialty pharmacy or standard pharmacy distribution TBD
Patient support via Janssen CarePath

2.2 Apremilast (Otezla) — Amgen (acquired from Celgene/BMS)
Mechanism of Action:
PDE4 inhibitor — oral, broad anti-inflammatory mechanism. Not targeted to IL-23 pathway specifically.
Key Efficacy Data:

PASI 75 at week 16: ~30-33% (ESTEEM-1) — significantly lower than biologics or ICOTYDE
PASI 90: ~10-15%
Well-tolerated; GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea) in early weeks

Market Position:

The only prior oral targeted therapy for psoriasis with meaningful market share
Modest efficacy limits it to mild-to-moderate psoriasis or biologic-intolerant patients
ICOTYDE directly displaces Otezla by offering biologic-class efficacy in the same oral format
Revenue declining as biologic competition intensifies; ICOTYDE will accelerate this

2.3 Deucravacitinib (Sotyktu) — BMS
Mechanism of Action:
Oral TYK2 inhibitor — allosteric, selective. Targets TYK2 (which mediates IL-23 and IL-12 signaling) without broadly inhibiting other JAK family members.
Key Efficacy Data:

POETYK PSO-1 (Phase 3): PASI 75: ~58% at week 16 vs. ~35% (apremilast) — superior to Otezla
PASI 90: ~37%
Positioned as "better than Otezla, safer than JAK inhibitors"

Market Position:

Closest oral competitor to ICOTYDE in psoriasis
Efficacy is better than Otezla but still substantially below injectable biologics and likely below ICOTYDE
No boxed warnings (unlike JAK inhibitors) — safety differentiation vs. JAK class
BMS promoting aggressively in dermatology

Relevance to ICOTYDE:

Direct oral-vs-oral competitor. The question is whether Sotyktu's efficacy ceiling (~PASI 90 of 37%) can compete with ICOTYDE's biologic-level PASI 90 rates
If ICOTYDE delivers PASI 90 >60%, Sotyktu's positioning is significantly weakened

2.4 Injectable IL-23 Inhibitors (TREMFYA, Skyrizi)
Relevance:

TREMFYA and Skyrizi are J&J and AbbVie's flagship injectable IL-23 agents (detailed in separate market context doc)
ICOTYDE and TREMFYA are J&J portfolio siblings — same pathway, different modalities
ICOTYDE may cannibalize some TREMFYA psoriasis share (patients switching from injection to oral), but J&J retains the revenue
TREMFYA retains differentiation via GI (UC, CD) and PsA indications — ICOTYDE is psoriasis-only currently

2.5 Injectable IL-17 Inhibitors (Cosentyx, Taltz, Bimzelx)
Relevance:

IL-17 inhibitors offer the highest skin clearance rates (Bimzelx PASI 100 ~60-70%)
ICOTYDE competes on convenience (oral) rather than pure efficacy ceiling
IL-17 inhibitors cannot be used in patients with IBD comorbidity — ICOTYDE (IL-23 pathway) has no such restriction
The oral convenience of ICOTYDE may attract patients who would otherwise be prescribed IL-17 agents

First-in-class oral IL-23 receptor antagonist — no other oral therapy delivers biologic-class IL-23 pathway blockade
Biologic-level efficacy in a pill — the core breakthrough; eliminates the efficacy-convenience trade-off
$5B+ peak revenue opportunity — reflects the enormous unmet demand for an effective oral psoriasis therapy
No injection burden: Eliminates needles, cold chain, clinic visits, injection training
J&J portfolio complementarity: TREMFYA (injectable multi-TA) + ICOTYDE (oral psoriasis) = comprehensive IL-23 portfolio
Pediatric inclusion (>=12 years): An oral biologic-class option for adolescents is a significant differentiator

Vulnerabilities:

Psoriasis-only indication (currently): TREMFYA and Skyrizi cover PsA and IBD; ICOTYDE cannot address patients with significant joint disease or GI comorbidity
New-to-market: No long-term safety data or real-world evidence; dermatologists will want to see post-marketing safety before switching established biologic patients
Pricing and payer coverage: Payer strategy during launch is critical; if priced at biologic levels, payers may resist covering an oral when cheaper oral alternatives (Otezla, Sotyktu) exist
Daily dosing compliance: Unlike Q8-12W injectable biologics, ICOTYDE requires daily adherence — a compliance concern for some patients
Sotyktu competition: BMS will aggressively defend the oral psoriasis space; expect head-to-head messaging on efficacy, safety, and physician familiarity
Injectable biologic stickiness: Patients well-controlled on TREMFYA or Skyrizi may not switch; ICOTYDE's primary growth is from new-to-systemic patients and injection-averse populations


4. Market Context
4.1 Market Size and Segmentation

US psoriasis systemic therapy market: ~$15-18B annually
Injectable biologic share: ~$12-14B (dominant)
Oral systemic share: ~$2-4B (Otezla + Sotyktu + traditional orals)
Biologic-eligible but untreated/undertreated: ~1-2 million patients — ICOTYDE's biggest growth opportunity
Injection-averse patients: ~10-20% of biologic-eligible population — a captive audience for oral IL-23

4.2 Target Prescriber Universe

Dermatologists: ~12,000 in the US; ~3,000-5,000 actively prescribe systemic psoriasis therapy
High-volume psoriasis specialists: ~500-1,000 at academic centers and psoriasis-focused practices
Primary care physicians: Manage mild psoriasis but refer moderate-to-severe; an oral option may encourage some to initiate systemic therapy without referral
Key dynamic: ICOTYDE lowers the prescribing threshold — dermatologists who hesitated to start patients on injectables may feel more comfortable starting with an oral biologic-class agent

4.3 Key Market Dynamics

Oral-switch wave is coming: ICOTYDE creates a new category — oral biologic-equivalent therapy. Expect significant patient interest and pull demand. The launch trajectory will be one of the fastest in psoriasis history
J&J dual-brand strategy (TREMFYA + ICOTYDE): J&J must balance two products targeting the same pathway. Expect positioning as: ICOTYDE for oral-preference/new-to-systemic patients; TREMFYA for patients wanting less frequent dosing and multi-domain coverage (PsA, IBD)
BMS counter-attack with Sotyktu: BMS will position Sotyktu as the proven oral option with 3+ years of data vs. ICOTYDE's new launch. Expect aggressive head-to-head messaging on safety track record
Payer dynamics will shape launch speed: If payers require step-through Otezla/Sotyktu before ICOTYDE, the ramp is slower. If ICOTYDE achieves first-line oral coverage, the ramp is explosive
Real-world evidence demand: Dermatologists want post-marketing data before broadly switching; KOL early adopters will generate the first RWE signal

4.4 Prescribing Decision Drivers

Efficacy vs. oral comparators: If ICOTYDE delivers PASI 90 rates >>50% (vs. Sotyktu ~37%, Otezla ~10-15%), the efficacy gap is enormous and the primary prescribing driver
Patient preference for oral: For new-to-systemic patients, oral is overwhelmingly preferred; for injection-averse patients, ICOTYDE removes the last barrier
Safety profile: Long-term safety data will take time to accumulate; dermatologists will weigh the known safety of injectable IL-23 vs. the new oral profile
Payer coverage and cost: Formulary positioning, prior authorization requirements, and copay relative to injectables will influence adoption speed
Multi-domain disease: Patients with PsA or IBD comorbidity may still need TREMFYA/Skyrizi for multi-domain coverage; ICOTYDE alone won't address joint or GI disease
Compliance: Daily pill vs. Q8-12W injection — some physicians prefer the guaranteed adherence of infrequent injections; others prefer the patient autonomy of oral dosing

6. Key Themes for Tracking
6.1 Primary Promotional Narratives



Brand
Core Message
Supporting Data




ICOTYDE
"First oral biologic-class therapy for psoriasis — biologic efficacy in a pill"
Biologic-level PASI 90 rates; first oral IL-23R antagonist


Sotyktu

POETYK data; established safety track record

10+ years on market; declining relevance

Highest injectable IL-23 efficacy and longest interval



6.2 Anticipated Competitive Battlegrounds

ICOTYDE vs. Sotyktu efficacy gap: The defining competition in oral psoriasis. If ICOTYDE delivers PASI 90 rates 2-3x higher than Sotyktu, the clinical differentiation is decisive. Track head-to-head physician perception and prescribing intent
Oral vs. injectable preference evolution: Track how many patients and physicians choose oral ICOTYDE over injectable biologics — is it primarily new-to-systemic patients, or are established biologic patients switching?
J&J TREMFYA → ICOTYDE cannibalization: Monitor whether TREMFYA psoriasis patients switch to ICOTYDE, and whether J&J's dual-brand messaging effectively segments the two products
Payer and access dynamics: Track formulary positioning, prior authorization requirements, and step therapy mandates — these will define launch speed
Safety signal monitoring: As the first oral IL-23R antagonist, any unexpected safety signals will disproportionately impact ICOTYDE adoption. Track dermatologist safety perceptions wave-over-wave
Real-world evidence accumulation: First RWE from early adopters will be critical for broader adoption. Track KOL sentiment and published case series

Approved for moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis (adults + pediatric >=12 years, >=40 kg)
J&J projects >$5 billion peak annual revenue
Mechanism: selective oral IL-23 receptor antagonist

Safety profile details — verify against prescribing information once widely available
